intro: Inventory of Terms The Inventory of Terms is designed to facilitate the use of the Syntopicon as a reference book. By means of the terms listed below in alphabetical order, the Inventory directs us to the relevant topics in the 102 chapters and thus enables us to discover what the Great Books have to say on the 3,000 topics comprising the themes of the great conversation. the titles of the 102 chapters are included in this alphabetical list because each great idea is also discussed in chapters other than the one devoted to its consideration . In order to see the particular topic in the context of related topics, the reader should turn first to the Outline of Topics for the idea in which the particular topic appears. the Outline of Topics in each chapter immediately precedes the Reference section, where the particular topics are listed with citations to the relevant passages from the Great Books.

Each term in the Inventory is accompanied by references to the chapters and topics in which the term is a significant element. the terms are the words or phrases printed in boldface type. Whole chapters are cited by both chapter number and name; for example, see CH 4: Art. Individual topics are cited by the name of the chapter and the number of the topic in the Outline of Topics in that chapter; for example, see Art 5a. In some cases the Inventory listing might be a cross-reference to other places in the Inventory. Cross-references are also in boldface type. In each entry topics are cited in the alphabetical order of the chapters in which they appear. However, whole chapters, when cited, always precede the individual topics. In some entries the citations are divided into two groups, those of primary and those of secondary significance. the first group is separated from the second by a diagonal line, followed by see also. Some words carry qualifiers in parentheses to indicate the distinct term being used. For example, Corruption (moral) provides references that apply to corruption in the moral or ethical sense, whereas Corruption (phys.) pertains to biological decay or decomposition, and Corruption (pol.) to abuses of power in the sphere of politics. Terms that are the names of particular sciences or fields of knowledge, such as ethics or mathematics, may be related to topics that treat the nature of the science itself or to topics concerned with the subject matter of that science. the words “science of” or “subject matter of” following such terms indicate which sort of topic is being cited. Phrases are sometimes necessary to express a single term or to narrow its meaning; for example, Law of contradiction or Military strategy and tactics. the same purpose is served by pairing related or similar words; for example, Appearance and reality, Franchise or suffrage, and History, historian. For more on the use of the Inventory of Terms, see the Introduction in Volume 1.
